items.q: What is the difference between physical and economic water scarcity?
items.correct: Physical scarcity is insufficient water supply for demand (e.g. arid regions); economic scarcity is inability to access available water due to lack of infrastructure or investment, despite water being present.
items.misconception: Water scarcity only happens in dry countries.
items.random_wrong: Economic scarcity means water is too expensive for governments to buy.
items.why: Sub-Saharan Africa has economic scarcity — there's water but no pipes, pumps or treatment. Students focus on rainfall; the geographical insight is that scarcity is partly an infrastructure/governance issue.

items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.q: Which line divides upland and lowland Britain?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Tees-Exe Line. “Watford Line” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where are softer sedimentary rocks found in UK?
items.why: Correct answer: Southeast England. “Scottish Highlands” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many km of coastline does the UK have?
items.why: Exact answer: 17820 km. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
